heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
retrieved.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

retrieved.text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
retrieved.text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

retrieved.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
retrieved.text:
u:ai enables the creation of so-called tailored AIs. These are individually configured chatbots that permanently store your instructions.

- Such chatbots can optionally work with their own knowledge base (e.g., guidelines, templates), which is then always given priority. This significantly reduces [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268300).
- Once you have built a chatbot, you can also share it with other people.

This means that recurring tasks can be handled more efficiently without having to explain what the AI should do each time.

In certain modules, an Internet search can be activated as an option. This accesses external information via the [Perplexity](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perplexity.ai) service. This gives you access to up-to-date information.

When Internet search is activated, the entire chat history to date is used as context. This means that personal or confidential data used in this chat leaves the closed system of u:ai. Data protection compliance is then no longer fully guaranteed.

Therefore, never use personal or confidential content in the same chat as the Internet search, but start a new chat for the Internet search.

When using AI in higher education, it is not only functionality that plays a role, but above all the legal and data protection framework in which a tool is operated. U:ai was developed specifically for the university context and differs in key respects from freely accessible, commercial AI offerings.

retrieved.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
retrieved.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

retrieved.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
retrieved.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

retrieved.title: Things AI is not recommended for
retrieved.text:
While AI has demonstrated remarkable capabilities, there are areas where its use is not recommended, as it could potentially compromise the autonomy of your scientific work or the integrity of your learning processes.

AI-generated summaries can provide an overview, but it is important to remember that they do not replace your own engagement with the material. Please note that AI-generated summaries are often also inadequate in terms of content, as they may omit essential aspects or distort statements from the original.

Recommendation: Engage with the topic yourself first or read the texts and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions, for example to clarify aspects you have not understood.

Tip: Do not use AI as a substitute for reading, but as a reading coach.

AI can support you in reading academic texts actively, critically and in a structured manner. For instance, it can provide guiding questions, suggestions for highlighting central arguments, or explanations of individual terms. It is recommended that you read the text yourself first and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions. This will help you to close gaps in understanding or to better contextualise argumentative structures (see tips on [prompting](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)).

Further information: [AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
reranked.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

reranked.title: Citing and labelling AI
reranked.text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

reranked.title: Citing and labelling AI
reranked.text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

reranked.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
reranked.text:
u:ai enables the creation of so-called tailored AIs. These are individually configured chatbots that permanently store your instructions.

- Such chatbots can optionally work with their own knowledge base (e.g., guidelines, templates), which is then always given priority. This significantly reduces [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268300).
- Once you have built a chatbot, you can also share it with other people.

This means that recurring tasks can be handled more efficiently without having to explain what the AI should do each time.

In certain modules, an Internet search can be activated as an option. This accesses external information via the [Perplexity](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perplexity.ai) service. This gives you access to up-to-date information.

When Internet search is activated, the entire chat history to date is used as context. This means that personal or confidential data used in this chat leaves the closed system of u:ai. Data protection compliance is then no longer fully guaranteed.

Therefore, never use personal or confidential content in the same chat as the Internet search, but start a new chat for the Internet search.

When using AI in higher education, it is not only functionality that plays a role, but above all the legal and data protection framework in which a tool is operated. U:ai was developed specifically for the university context and differs in key respects from freely accessible, commercial AI offerings.

It was used in particular to shorten extensive texts and to simplify language, making content easier to understand and more accessible. The AI was used as a supporting tool, while content responsibility and quality review remained with the authors. The use of AI is based on the [Guidelines on AI in Studies and Teaching](https://doi.org/10.25365/phaidra.544), which aim to ensure a responsible and transparent use of AI applications in an academic context.

reranked.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
reranked.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

reranked.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
reranked.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

reranked.title: Things AI is not recommended for
reranked.text:
While AI has demonstrated remarkable capabilities, there are areas where its use is not recommended, as it could potentially compromise the autonomy of your scientific work or the integrity of your learning processes.

AI-generated summaries can provide an overview, but it is important to remember that they do not replace your own engagement with the material. Please note that AI-generated summaries are often also inadequate in terms of content, as they may omit essential aspects or distort statements from the original.

Recommendation: Engage with the topic yourself first or read the texts and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions, for example to clarify aspects you have not understood.

Tip: Do not use AI as a substitute for reading, but as a reading coach.

AI can support you in reading academic texts actively, critically and in a structured manner. For instance, it can provide guiding questions, suggestions for highlighting central arguments, or explanations of individual terms. It is recommended that you read the text yourself first and then use AI for targeted follow-up questions. This will help you to close gaps in understanding or to better contextualise argumentative structures (see tips on [prompting](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)).

Further information: [AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

selected.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
selected.text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
selected.text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

selected.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
selected.text:
u:ai enables the creation of so-called tailored AIs. These are individually configured chatbots that permanently store your instructions.

- Such chatbots can optionally work with their own knowledge base (e.g., guidelines, templates), which is then always given priority. This significantly reduces [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268300).
- Once you have built a chatbot, you can also share it with other people.

This means that recurring tasks can be handled more efficiently without having to explain what the AI should do each time.

In certain modules, an Internet search can be activated as an option. This accesses external information via the [Perplexity](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perplexity.ai) service. This gives you access to up-to-date information.

When Internet search is activated, the entire chat history to date is used as context. This means that personal or confidential data used in this chat leaves the closed system of u:ai. Data protection compliance is then no longer fully guaranteed.

Therefore, never use personal or confidential content in the same chat as the Internet search, but start a new chat for the Internet search.

When using AI in higher education, it is not only functionality that plays a role, but above all the legal and data protection framework in which a tool is operated. U:ai was developed specifically for the university context and differs in key respects from freely accessible, commercial AI offerings.

selected.title: Studying safely
selected.text:
No smoking.

The only thing that is smoking here are our brains.

It is prohibited to handle flames or flammable liquids.

Drinking enough water will keep you fit and alert.

Access restrictions: stay away from the marked hazard zones.

Icon: Use the University’s study spaces and Student Spaces. (student while reading)

Use the University’s study spaces and Student Spaces.

Animals are not allowed inside. Exceptions apply to guide, assistance and other service dogs.

You can bring along your lucky charm.

Be quiet.

Science-based, respectful and appreciative discussions are expressly wanted.

Sports equipment may not be used inside the buildings.

Please train your mind as much as you want here.

It is prohibited to wear, carry or bring weapons of any kind to the University.

A razor-sharp mind is the only weapon allowed.

In addition to the security management team and the porters, you can also contact the [StudiesServiceCenters (SSC)/StudiesServiceUnits (SSS)](https://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/), your [teachers](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/en/index.html) and the employees of the different organisational units in case of incidents.
